items.question: Who needs to understand model complexity when building ML systems?
items.answer: ML engineers, data scientists, and researchers who select algorithms and hyperparameters — anyone choosing tree depth, polynomial degree, network size, or regularization strength to hit validation targets without overfitting.

items.question: What is model complexity in the bias–variance framework?
items.answer: Model complexity (capacity) is how flexibly a hypothesis class can fit data — controlled by knobs like depth, degree, width, or k. It is the main lever that typically lowers bias and raises variance as you increase it.

items.question: When should you increase model complexity during tuning?
items.answer: Increase complexity when both training and validation error are high — underfitting from high bias — and a richer model substantially lowers training error without an unacceptable train–validation gap.

items.question: Where does the validation-error sweet spot appear on a complexity sweep?
items.answer: On a validation curve (error vs depth, degree, etc.), the sweet spot is near the U-curve minimum — moderate complexity between the high-bias left (underfitting) and high-variance right (overfitting).

items.question: Why not always use the most complex model you can afford?
items.answer: Past the validation minimum, extra complexity mainly fits training noise — variance rises and validation error increases. More compute does not remove overfitting on finite, noisy data.

items.question: How do you pick complexity in practice?
items.answer: Start with a simple baseline, sweep one complexity hyperparameter with cross-validation, plot train vs validation metrics, and choose the simplest setting whose validation error is near the minimum.

items.question: Low complexity vs high complexity — how do train and validation errors differ?
items.answer: Low complexity (underfitting): both training and validation error are high — high bias. High complexity (overfitting): training error is low but validation error is much higher — large train–val gap from high variance.

items.question: Nominal parameter count vs effective complexity — what is the difference?
items.answer: Parameter count is raw model size; effective complexity includes regularization, early stopping, and data limits. A heavily regularized high-degree polynomial can behave like a low-degree model on validation — parameter count alone misleads selection.

items.question: Cause: polynomial degree increased from 2 to 15 on 60 noisy points. Effect?
items.answer: Training error drops sharply, bias falls, but validation error rises after a point — the model wiggles through noise (high variance). Optimal degree sits between the underfit and overfit extremes.

items.question: Cause: dataset grows 100× with the same model family. Effect?
items.answer: Variance shrinks with more data — the validation-error minimum shifts toward higher complexity. A depth that overfit at 1k rows may be optimal at 100k rows without changing the algorithm.
